title: JOIN: Combining Readings With What They Mean
learningObjectives: Combine two related tables with an INNER JOIN
Use a LEFT JOIN to keep rows even when a matching related row doesn't exist
Choose the correct join type based on whether missing matches should be dropped or kept
Avoid joining on the wrong column and silently duplicating or losing rows
whyItMatters: A readings table with a bare source_id column doesn't tell you which substation that ID refers to, its location, or its rated capacity. That information lives in a separate sources table, and joining is the only way to combine a reading with what it actually means.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's ability to answer questions that span both readings and metadata — e.g. 'which substations exceeded 90% of their rated capacity today' — which requires combining two tables, not just querying one.

problem.summary: A source_id alone doesn't tell you a substation's rated capacity.
problem.content: Your readings table has a `source_id` column like `'substation-12'`, but nothing about that ID tells you the substation's rated capacity, which you need to know whether 4821 kW is actually close to overload for that specific substation.

motivation.summary: Related data lives in separate tables and needs a reliable way to reconnect.
motivation.content: Real data almost never lives in one flat table — normalizing related information into separate tables (readings vs. substations) avoids duplicating the substation's details on every single reading row, but it means you need a reliable way to bring them back together when a question spans both.

theory.summary: INNER JOIN keeps only matches; LEFT JOIN keeps every left row even without a match.
theory.content: `SELECT r.demand_kw, s.rated_capacity_kw FROM readings r JOIN sources s ON r.source_id = s.id;` combines rows from both tables wherever the ON condition matches. An INNER JOIN (the default `JOIN`) only keeps rows with a match on both sides. A LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table, filling in NULLs for the right side when no match exists — essential when a reading might reference a source that's since been decommissioned or hasn't been added to the sources table yet.

visualExplanation.summary: Two tables connect via matching IDs; INNER JOIN keeps connections, LEFT JOIN keeps unmatched left rows too.
visualExplanation.content: Two separate tables sit side by side connecting via matching ID values, with an INNER JOIN highlighting only the connected rows and a LEFT JOIN additionally including left-side rows with no connection, shown with a dashed NULL placeholder.

interactiveDemo.summary: Drag a join condition between two tables and toggle join types live.
interactiveDemo.content: Drag a join condition between two visualized tables and watch the resulting combined row set update live, toggling between INNER and LEFT JOIN to see which rows appear or disappear.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: join readings to sources and filter on the combined result.
guidedCoding.content:
```sql
SELECT r.timestamp, r.demand_kw, s.rated_capacity_kw
FROM readings r
JOIN sources s ON r.source_id = s.id
WHERE r.demand_kw > s.rated_capacity_kw * 0.9;
```

independentCoding.summary: Write a LEFT JOIN that surfaces readings with no matching source.
independentCoding.content: Write a LEFT JOIN query that lists every reading along with its source's rated capacity where known, and shows NULL for readings whose source isn't in the sources table at all.

debugging.summary: Fix a join condition using the wrong columns.
debugging.content: A query joins `ON r.id = s.id` instead of `ON r.source_id = s.id`, producing a nonsensical or massively duplicated result set. Find and fix the join condition.

reflection.summary: INNER JOIN can silently hide rows that LEFT JOIN would surface.
reflection.content: What would an INNER JOIN have silently hidden from you, compared to the LEFT JOIN version, if some readings referenced sources not yet in the sources table?

teachBack.summary: Explain when to choose LEFT JOIN over INNER JOIN.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone when you'd choose a LEFT JOIN over an INNER JOIN, using the decommissioned-substation scenario as the concrete example.

miniProject.summary: Answer a real cross-table question with a correct join.
miniProject.content: Extend your Mission 2 SQL queries to answer: 'which substations exceeded 90% of their rated capacity at any point today,' requiring a correct join between readings and sources.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the first feature reasoning across two related tables.
portfolioEvidence.content: This join-based query is the first Digital Twin feature that reasons across two related tables instead of one — the shape most real monitoring questions take.

labs.title: Readings & Sources
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise joining a readings table to a sources table, including a deliberately orphaned reading requiring a correct LEFT JOIN to surface.

simulations.title: Join Type Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows two tables connecting via matching keys, toggling live between INNER and LEFT JOIN to reveal which rows appear or disappear.

flashcards.front: What happens to a reading whose source_id has no matching row in sources, under an INNER JOIN vs. a LEFT JOIN?
flashcards.back: Under an INNER JOIN, that reading disappears entirely from the result. Under a LEFT JOIN, it's kept, with NULL in place of the missing source columns.

flashcards.front: What usually goes wrong when a JOIN's ON condition uses the wrong columns?
flashcards.back: The join either matches far too many row combinations (massive, meaningless duplication) or far too few (rows that should match don't), depending on which mismatched columns happen to coincide.

rubric: Correctly chooses INNER vs. LEFT JOIN based on whether unmatched rows should be dropped or kept
Join condition uses the correct matching columns, verified against row counts before and after
Mini-project query correctly answers the stated cross-table question
